trade.summary: Korea-Poland trade has grown substantially, bolstered by major Korean defense and industrial investments. Korea exports electronics (Samsung, LG operate Polish factories), automobiles, and defense equipment to Poland. Poland exports machinery components, food products, and chemicals to Korea. Both benefit from the EU-Korea FTA (2011).
top_products_a_to_b: consumer electronics (Samsung, LG factories in Poland)

diplomatic.summary: Korea-Poland relations have strengthened dramatically since 2022, driven by Poland's massive defense procurement program (responding to Russia's Ukraine invasion) and Korea's emergence as a top-tier defense exporter. Poland signed the largest-ever Korean arms deal — worth tens of billions — including K2 tanks, K9 howitzers, FA-50 aircraft, and Chunmoo rocket artillery. This defense partnership has elevated bilateral relations to strategic partnership level.
recent_incidents: Poland-Korea mega defense procurement deal (2022-2023): K2 tanks, K9 howitzers, FA-50 jets, Chunmoo MLRS
Growing strategic dialogue following defense partnership elevation

military.summary: The Poland-Korea defense relationship is one of Korea's most significant bilateral defense partnerships. Poland has contracted to purchase 1,000+ K2 tanks, 672 K9 howitzers, 48 FA-50 aircraft, and 288 Chunmoo MLRS units — the largest arms deal in Korean history. Poland is undertaking licensed production of some systems domestically. Both countries view Russian aggression as an existential threat (Poland directly, Korea analogously through North Korea). Korea-Poland share a unique 'island' defense mentality.
military.alliance_status: Strategic defense partners — Poland NATO, Korea US ally

financial.summary: Korean FDI in Poland has grown significantly, led by Samsung (electronics factory in Wronki), LG (appliance manufacturing), and defense industry investments. The massive defense contracts create long-term financial flows and technology transfer commitments. Polish government bonds and equities have limited Korean institutional exposure.

energy.summary: Limited direct bilateral energy trade. Korea is exploring participation in Poland's nuclear energy expansion. Both countries are working on energy diversification and security. Some cooperation on LNG supply chain given Poland's new LNG terminal.

scientific.summary: Growing scientific cooperation particularly in defense technology, nuclear energy, and electronics. Defense industrial cooperation under the K2/K9 deals involves significant technology transfer to Polish defense firms. Academic exchanges are expanding, particularly in engineering.
